one_line_takeaway: By teaching an LLM a learned, context-conditioned semantic steganography scheme via fine-tuning-data poisoning rather than a fixed lexical trigger, CORDYCEPS enables covert prompt-injection (UCC, >71% ASVh) and covert data-exfiltration (BCC, 78-93% covert accuracy) attacks that evade outlier detectors, prompt-injection classifiers, and unlearning/alignment defenses (ONION, DataSentinel, PromptGuard, PromptLocate, CROW, SecAlign) far more effectively than existing fixed-trigger backdoor and heuristic prompt-injection baselines.
key_finding: UCC achieves ASVh > 0.71 across all 5 models (e.g., 0.712 on LLA3-8B vs. 0.392 for the CoA baseline on the same clean fine-tuned model, an approximately 80% relative improvement), and clearly outperforms all five re-implemented backdoor baselines (e.g., Sleeper Agent reaches only 0.257 ASVh on gemma-3-12B). BCC achieves covert data-exfiltration accuracy of 0.78-0.93 across models (peaking at 0.930 on Qwen3-30B) versus near-zero (<=0.06) for Base/Clean models queried directly under a keyword filter. Under a 1% poisoning rate (with absolute poisoned-example count held constant via a larger 10,000-example training set), BCC covert accuracy stays between 0.760 and 0.920 with the Qwen3.6-35B oracle. On stealth: CORDYCEPS achieves only 0.063 TPR at 5% FPR under the ONION outlier detector (vs. 0.661-0.718 for fixed-trigger MTBA/CTBA baselines), averages 0.005 detection rate under PromptGuard (vs. 1.000 for CoA), and evades PromptLocate payload localization (ROUGE-L/embedding-similarity scores far below CoA's >0.9). On robustness to defense/unlearning: CROW degrades UCC effectiveness by only 5.75% on gemma-3-12B (vs. 13.31% for VPI), and SecAlign has negligible effect on UCC (Delta < 0.02 on PHI4-15B, even a slight positive effect on gemma-3-12B) despite strongly suppressing CoA (Delta = -76.5% on LLA3-8B). Utility is largely preserved: clean vs. poisoned models differ by at most about 1.5% across the five utility benchmarks, with MMLU degradation under 0.3% on average.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CORDYCEPS, a data-poisoning algorithm for LLM fine-tuning datasets that teaches a victim model a learned, context-conditioned steganographic hiding scheme called Semantic Hiding under Shared knowledge (SHuSh), rather than a fixed lexical trigger. CORDYCEPS pairs attacker-chosen plaintext instructions with 'semantic anchors' (short encyclopedic facts, e.g. Wikipedia entries via WikiDes) and uses an oracle LLM (default Gemini 2.5 Pro) in a multi-phase, one-shot-prompted pipeline: (1) generate anchor-consistent stegotexts that encode the plaintext, (2) separately generate chain-of-thought encoding/decoding reasoning traces to teach the semantic mapping generatively rather than by memorization, and (3) format the results as supervised fine-tuning examples. This induces two new attack primitives the authors call covert control attacks: Unidirectional Covert Control (UCC), where an attacker embeds a malicious instruction as a stegotext inside untrusted retrieved <data> (an indirect-prompt-injection-style vector), and Bidirectional Covert Control (BCC), where the poisoned model both receives a stegotext-encoded query and encodes its (e.g., PII/financial) response as a stegotext, achieving covert data exfiltration. The paper also formalizes the scheme's desired properties (stealth, robustness, encoding/decoding accuracy) via a theoretical framework using a Semantic Consistency Score (cosine similarity between anchor/message embeddings), which it validates empirically in an appendix-level feasibility analysis.
threat_model: The attacker can only construct/inject a poisoned fine-tuning dataset (Dpoison); they have no access to or control over the base model architecture, weights, or training process, and cannot modify inference-time decoding. The victim model trainer performs standard supervised fine-tuning on the poisoned dataset and is assumed to run automated outlier/data-level detection prior to training plus have a small held-out clean fine-tuning set (standard assumptions from prior poisoning-defense literature). An online defender is also assumed to monitor, filter, or paraphrase all LLM inputs and outputs, including the untrusted <data> field, at inference/deployment time. Within this model, the attacker's goal is either to hijack execution via UCC (append a stegotext-encoded malicious instruction to external/retrieved content) or to exfiltrate sensitive data via BCC (prompt the poisoned model so its output encodes sensitive information as a stegotext, evading output monitoring).
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation is that while exact anchor-identity matching between the attacker's poisoned anchors and test-time anchors is not required, SHuSh still depends on the victim model sharing overlapping semantic understanding of the chosen anchor topic (drawn from encyclopedic/Wikipedia-style knowledge); weak or highly domain-specific shared knowledge is described as a practical limitation on generalization. The oracle-dependence ablation shows attack quality depends materially on the oracle model's ability to form reliable semantic associations: a weaker oracle (Qwen3.5-9B) reduces BCC covert accuracy substantially (e.g., from 0.870 to 0.580 on Qwen3-30B) versus stronger oracles (Gemini 2.5 Pro, Qwen3.6-35B). An adaptive DataSentinel detector fine-tuned specifically on CORDYCEPS pairs raised some detection rates (e.g., 0.780 on grammar correction, 0.877 on summarization) but at the cost of high false positives on benign covertext (0.554 on grammar correction, 0.377 on hate detection), which the authors themselves characterize as 'not yet' a clean separation rather than a defeated defense. The attack requires fine-tuning-level dataset poisoning access (not a pure inference-time attack), and the threat model assumes the attacker has no control over the base model or training process beyond the poisoned data itself.

datasets_used: WikiDes (Wikipedia-based short-description dataset, used as common-knowledge semantic anchors)
OpenO1-SFT (source of 1,260 reasoning-domain clean fine-tuning examples, Dclean, plus 50 held-out clean test examples)
OpenPromptInjection benchmark (7 NLP tasks x 7 task pairs; UCC evaluation, 2,450 test samples)
Synthetic BCC dataset (200 exfiltration targets, e.g. PII/financial data, generated by Gemini 2.5 Pro; 100 poisoned + 900 clean = 1,000-sample training set, 100 held-out test)
MuSR, GSM8K, MMLU, IFEval, GPQA (utility/capability preservation benchmarks via lm-evaluation-harness)
quantitative_results: UCC ASVh 0.712 on LLA3-8B vs. CoA baseline 0.392 on clean fine-tuned model (~80% relative improvement)
UCC ASVh > 0.71 across all 5 models at 10% poisoning rate; ASVs-ASVh gap as small as 0.001 on Qwen3-4B
BCC covert accuracy 0.78-0.93 across models (0.930 peak on Qwen3-30B) vs. <=0.06 for Base/Clean models
At 1% poisoning rate (Qwen3.6-35B oracle), BCC covert accuracy remains 0.760-0.920
ONION detection: CORDYCEPS TPR 0.063 at 5% FPR vs. 0.661 (MTBA) and 0.718 (CTBA) for fixed-trigger backdoor baselines
PromptGuard average detection rate 0.005 for UCC vs. 1.000 for CoA
CROW degrades UCC by only 5.75% on gemma-3-12B vs. 13.31% degradation for VPI backdoor baseline
SecAlign reduces CoA effectiveness by -76.5% on LLA3-8B but UCC effectiveness change is < 0.02 (even +0.81% on gemma-3-12B)
Utility preserved within ~1.5% across 5 benchmarks; MMLU average degradation < 0.3%
Embedding-clustering separability of stegotext vs. covertext: silhouette score 0.014, between/within distance ratio 1.021, Fisher's ratio 0.0267 (near-inseparable)
baselines_compared: DataSentinel and adaptive DataSentinel prompt-injection detectors
